entry.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream. ## Execution Model Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal. The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited. CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags. ## Available Globals ### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values. Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: } IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself. ### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display() ### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `
` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value. ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values. Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(

You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream. ## Execution Model Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal. The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited. CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags. ## Available Globals ### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values. Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: } IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself. ### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display() ### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `
` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value. ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values. Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(

You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream. ## Execution Model Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal. The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited. CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags. ## Available Globals ### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values. Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: } IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself. ### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display() ### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `
` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value. ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values. Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(

Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal. The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited. CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags. ## Available Globals ### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values. Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: } IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself. ### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display() ### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `
` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value. ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values. Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(

field.name: content
lines
field.text: var words = TextUtils.wordCount("Hello world hello typescript hello")

entry.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream. ## Execution Model Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal. The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited. CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags. ## Available Globals ### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values. Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: } IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself. ### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display() ### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `
` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value. ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values. Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(

field.name: content
field.text: var words = TextUtils.wordCount("Hello world hello typescript hello") await stop(words)

entry.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream. ## Execution Model Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal. The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited. CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags. ## Available Globals ### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values. Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: } IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself. ### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display() ### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `
` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value. ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values. Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(

field.name: content
field.text: var title = TextUtils.titleCase("hello world typescript") await stop(title)

field.name: lines
field.text: var truncated = TextUtils.truncate("This is a very long text that needs to be truncated", 20)

entry.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream. ## Execution Model Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal. The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited. CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags. ## Available Globals ### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values. Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: } IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself. ### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display() ### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `
` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value. ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values. Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(

field.name: content
field.text: var truncated = TextUtils.truncate("This is a very long text that needs to be truncated", 20) await stop(truncated)

field.name: content
field.text: ← stop { truncated: "This is a very long…" }

field.name: lines
field.text: // All DataProcessor and TextUtils methods have been demonstrated successfully

field.name: lines
field.text: // The classes provide comprehensive data processing and text manipulation capabilities

field.name: lines
field.text: // No further execution needed - all methods have been tested with example data

field.name: content
field.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream. ## Execution Model Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal. The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited. CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags. ## Available Globals ### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values. Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: } IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself. ### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display() ### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `
` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value. ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values. Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(

field.name: content
content
field.text: var words = TextUtils.wordCount("Hello world hello typescript hello") await stop(words)

field.name: content
field.text: var title = TextUtils.titleCase("hello world typescript") await stop(title)

field.name: content
field.text: var truncated = TextUtils.truncate("This is a very long text that needs to be truncated", 20) await stop(truncated)

field.name: content
field.text: ← stop { truncated: "This is a very long…" }
